questions.question:
Describe the process of photosynthesis under the following headings:
(a) Definition
(b) Word equation and balanced chemical equation
(c) Factors affecting the rate of photosynthesis
(d) Importance of photosynthesis
questions.answer:
(a) Definition: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants use light energy (from the sun), carbon dioxide, and water to manufacture their own food (glucose) and release oxygen as a by-product.

(b) Word equation:
Carbon dioxide + Water → Glucose + Oxygen (in presence of light and chlorophyll)

Balanced equation:
6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

(c) Factors affecting rate:
• Light intensity — more light increases rate
• CO₂ concentration — more CO₂ increases rate
• Temperature — up to 40°C, higher temperature increases rate; above this enzymes denature
• Water availability — water is a raw material
• Chlorophyll content — more chlorophyll means higher rate

(d) Importance:
• Provides food for all organisms (directly or indirectly)
• Produces oxygen needed for respiration of all aerobic organisms
• Removes CO₂ from the atmosphere, reducing greenhouse effect
• Basis of all food chains and energy flow in ecosystems

questions.question:
Explain the structure and function of the human heart.
(a) Name the four chambers of the heart.
(b) Describe the pathway of blood through the heart.
(c) Explain the role of valves in the heart.
(d) Distinguish between the pulmonary and systemic circulation.
questions.answer:
(a) Four chambers:
• Right atrium
• Right ventricle
• Left atrium
• Left ventricle

(b) Pathway of blood:
Deoxygenated blood → Right atrium → Tricuspid valve → Right ventricle → Pulmonary valve → Pulmonary artery → Lungs (for oxygenation) → Pulmonary vein → Left atrium → Bicuspid/Mitral valve → Left ventricle → Aortic valve → Aorta → Body

(c) Role of valves:
Valves prevent the backflow of blood. They open when pressure on one side is greater and close when pressure reverses, ensuring blood flows in one direction only through the heart.

(d) Pulmonary circulation: The pathway of blood from the right ventricle to the lungs and back to the left atrium. Blood is deoxygenated going to lungs and oxygenated returning.

Systemic circulation: The pathway of blood from the left ventricle through the body tissues and back to the right atrium. Blood is oxygenated going out and deoxygenated returning.

questions.question:
Describe Mendel's experiment with pea plants, explaining the laws he derived.
(a) Explain the Law of Segregation with an example.
(b) In a cross between a homozygous tall plant (TT) and a homozygous short plant (tt), show the expected phenotype ratio in the F1 and F2 generations.
questions.answer:
(a) Law of Segregation: Mendel's first law states that an organism possesses two alleles for each trait. During gamete formation, these alleles separate (segregate) so that each gamete receives only one allele. When fertilisation occurs, the offspring receive one allele from each parent.

Example: A tall pea plant (TT) crossed with a short pea plant (tt) — the T and t alleles segregate during gamete formation. Each gamete carries either T or t, not both.

(b) F1 Generation:
Parents: TT × tt
Gametes: T × t
F1 offspring: All Tt
Phenotype: All tall (T is dominant)

F2 Generation (cross F1 × F1):
Parents: Tt × Tt
Gametes: T, t × T, t

Using a Punnett square:
     T    t
T   TT   Tt
t   Tt   tt

Genotype ratio: 1TT : 2Tt : 1tt
Phenotype ratio: 3 Tall : 1 Short

Therefore 75% tall and 25% short in F2.

questions.question:
Describe the process of aerobic respiration in living organisms.
(a) Write the overall equation.
(b) Describe the two main stages (glycolysis and Krebs cycle).
(c) Calculate the total ATP produced from one molecule of glucose.
(d) Compare aerobic and anaerobic respiration.
questions.answer:
(a) Overall equation:
C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + Energy (ATP)

(b) Two main stages:
Stage 1 — Glycolysis (in cytoplasm):
Glucose (6C) is broken down into two molecules of pyruvate (3C). This produces a net gain of 2 ATP and 2 NADH. No oxygen required.

Stage 2 — Krebs Cycle (in mitochondrial matrix):
Pyruvate is converted to Acetyl CoA and enters the Krebs cycle. CO₂ is released, and high-energy electron carriers (NADH and FADH₂) are produced. These pass electrons to the electron transport chain, driving ATP synthesis.

(c) Total ATP yield:
Glycolysis: 2 ATP
Krebs cycle: 2 ATP
Electron transport chain: 32 ATP
Total: approximately 36-38 ATP per glucose molecule

(d) Comparison:
Aerobic: Requires oxygen; produces CO₂ and H₂O; yields ~38 ATP; occurs in mitochondria.
Anaerobic: No oxygen required; produces lactic acid (animals) or ethanol + CO₂ (yeast); yields only 2 ATP; occurs in cytoplasm.

questions.question:
Explain the concept of homeostasis in humans, using body temperature regulation as an example.
(a) Define homeostasis.
(b) Describe how the body responds when body temperature rises above normal.
(c) Describe how the body responds when body temperature falls below normal.
(d) What is the role of the hypothalamus?
questions.answer:
(a) Homeostasis is the ability of organisms to maintain a constant internal environment despite changes in the external environment. It involves negative feedback mechanisms that detect deviations from the set point and initiate corrective responses.

(b) When body temperature rises:
• Thermoreceptors in the skin and hypothalamus detect the rise.
• The hypothalamus sends signals to effectors.
• Sweat glands produce more sweat — evaporation cools the skin.
• Blood vessels near the skin surface dilate (vasodilation) — more blood flows to the surface, radiating more heat.
• Body hairs lie flat — reducing the insulating layer of trapped air.
• Result: Body temperature returns to normal (37°C).

(c) When body temperature falls:
• Thermoreceptors detect the drop.
• Sweat glands reduce activity.
• Blood vessels near skin constrict (vasoconstriction) — less blood to skin surface, conserving heat.
• Muscles shiver — generating heat through rapid contractions.
• Body hairs stand erect (piloerection) — trapping more air for insulation.
• Result: Body temperature returns to normal.

(d) The hypothalamus acts as the thermostat of the body. It constantly monitors blood temperature and compares it to the set point (37°C). It coordinates the responses of all the effectors to restore normal temperature through negative feedback.

questions.question:
Describe the nitrogen cycle, explaining the roles of different types of bacteria.
(a) Draw and annotate the nitrogen cycle.
(b) Explain the role of each of the following in the cycle:
    i. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria
    ii. Nitrifying bacteria
    iii. Denitrifying bacteria
    iv. Decomposers
questions.answer:
(a) Nitrogen Cycle (description of diagram):
Nitrogen gas (N₂) in atmosphere → [Nitrogen fixation] → Ammonia/ammonium (NH₄⁺) → [Nitrification] → Nitrites (NO₂⁻) → Nitrates (NO₃⁻) → [Taken up by plants] → Plant protein → [Eaten by animals] → Animal protein → [Death/excretion] → [Decomposition] → Ammonia → [back to nitrification or] → [Denitrification] → N₂ gas

(b) Roles:
i. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria (e.g. Rhizobium in legume root nodules, Azotobacter in soil):
Convert atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) into ammonia (NH₃) or ammonium ions (NH₄⁺) that plants can absorb. This makes nitrogen available to living organisms.

ii. Nitrifying bacteria (e.g. Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter):
Nitrosomonas convert ammonium ions to nitrites (NO₂⁻).
Nitrobacter convert nitrites to nitrates (NO₃⁻).
Nitrates are the form of nitrogen most easily absorbed by plant roots.

iii. Denitrifying bacteria (e.g. Pseudomonas denitrificans):
Convert nitrates back to nitrogen gas (N₂) and release it into the atmosphere. This reduces soil fertility but completes the nitrogen cycle.

iv. Decomposers (bacteria and fungi):
Break down dead organic matter (dead plants, animals, faeces) into ammonia and ammonium ions through the process of ammonification. This returns nitrogen compounds to the soil.

questions.question:
Explain DNA replication and protein synthesis.
(a) Describe the process of DNA replication.
(b) Distinguish between transcription and translation.
(c) Explain what is meant by the genetic code.
questions.answer:
(a) DNA Replication (Semi-conservative):
1. The double helix unwinds and the two strands separate (helicase enzyme breaks hydrogen bonds).
2. Each original strand serves as a template.
3. Free DNA nucleotides align opposite their complementary bases on each template strand (A-T, G-C).
4. DNA polymerase joins the new nucleotides together, forming two new identical DNA double helices.
5. Each new molecule has one original (parent) strand and one new strand — hence 'semi-conservative'.

(b) Transcription vs Translation:
Transcription: Occurs in the nucleus. DNA template is used to produce a complementary mRNA strand. mRNA then leaves the nucleus.

Translation: Occurs at ribosomes in the cytoplasm. The mRNA sequence is read in codons (groups of 3 bases). Transfer RNA (tRNA) molecules bring the appropriate amino acids. Amino acids are joined by peptide bonds to form a polypeptide chain (protein).

(c) The Genetic Code:
The genetic code is the relationship between the sequence of bases in DNA/mRNA and the sequence of amino acids in a protein. It consists of codons — triplets of bases. Each codon specifies a particular amino acid or a stop signal. The code is:
• Universal (same in all living organisms)
• Degenerate (more than one codon can code for the same amino acid)
• Non-overlapping (each base is read only once)

questions.question:
Discuss the human immune system.
(a) Distinguish between specific and non-specific immunity.
(b) Explain the role of antibodies in immune response.
(c) Explain the principle behind vaccination.
(d) Distinguish between active and passive immunity.
questions.answer:
(a) Non-specific immunity:
The body's first line of defence — does not distinguish between different pathogens.
Examples: Skin barrier, mucus, cilia, stomach acid, inflammatory response, phagocytosis by neutrophils.

Specific immunity:
Targeted response against a specific pathogen involving lymphocytes. Produces memory cells for future protection. Slower on first exposure but faster on subsequent exposure.

(b) Role of antibodies:
Antibodies are proteins (immunoglobulins) produced by B-lymphocytes in response to a specific antigen. They have a specific shape that matches the antigen.
Functions:
• Agglutination — clumping pathogens together for phagocytosis
• Neutralisation — blocking toxins or viral binding sites
• Opsonisation — coating pathogens to make them easier for phagocytes to engulf

(c) Principle of vaccination:
A vaccine contains weakened/dead pathogens or their antigens. When introduced, the immune system mounts a primary response — producing antibodies and memory cells. If the real pathogen later enters the body, memory cells rapidly produce large quantities of antibodies, destroying the pathogen before disease develops.

(d) Active vs Passive immunity:
Active immunity: The body produces its own antibodies (natural — after infection; artificial — after vaccination). Slow to develop but long-lasting due to memory cells.

Passive immunity: Antibodies are given from an external source (natural — antibodies from mother through breast milk/placenta; artificial — injection of antibodies). Immediate protection but short-lived as no memory cells are formed.
